column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Stainless Steel 304 Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/metal/alloy/stainless-steel-304-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for metal oxide removal
Near-IR wavelength for optimal metal absorption
Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution
Energy per unit area for oxide removal
Pulse duration for minimal heat-affected zone
Beam travel speed for uniform treatment
Number of cleaning cycles for complete removal

Typical range for industrial 1064 nm pulsed fiber lasers cleaning SS304 contaminants like oils or rust; starting at 200 W balances efficiency and safety to avoid melting (melting point ~1425°C). Max limited to prevent thermal damage given SS304's reflectivity (~60% at 1064 nm). Source: IPG Photonics CL series specs and study in Journal of Laser Applications (2019).

Effective range for contaminant removal without substrate ablation; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., 500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on SS304 to minimize heat accumulation due to its moderate thermal conductivity (16 W/mK). Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals and Applied Surface Science (2020).

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective industrial cleaning of SS304; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) reduce HAZ but are less common. 30 ns minimizes thermal effects while ablating contaminants. Source: Laser physics research in Optics Express (2017) on ablation thresholds for metals.

Typical for 1064 nm cleaning to achieve uniform energy delivery; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk heat buildup on reflective SS304. 30 kHz recommended for balance. Trade-off: faster cleaning vs. potential oxidation. Source: Academic literature in Lasers in Engineering (2021).

Fluence range for safe contaminant removal (e.g., rust, grease) without damaging SS304; damage threshold ~4 J/cm² for ns pulses at 1064 nm. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² avoids ablation while effective, considering high reflectivity. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (2018).

Beam diameter range for cleaning; smaller for fine details (50-100 μm), larger for broad areas (200-300 μm). 150 μm recommended for SS304 industrial prep to ensure even fluence distribution. Source: Equipment specs from Adapt Laser Systems and Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2019).

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (up to 5) for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on SS304 without excessive heat input. Source: Industry case studies in Journal of Cleaner Production (2022) on metal surface preparation.

Ensures uniform coverage and avoids gaps/striping; 60% recommended for SS304 cleaning to optimize energy overlap without overexposure. Typical 30-80% based on scan patterns. Source: Process optimization in Applied Physics A (2020).
